lesson:
If you already know 動うごく (うごく, to move — intransitive), this is its transitive partner. The pattern is clean: 動うごく = it moves on its own. 動うごかす = you make it move. That 〜かす ending is a productive causative suffix in Japanese — you'll see it in other pairs like 飛とぶ/飛とばす, 沸くわく/沸かすわかす. Recognizing the pattern saves you from treating each pair as separate vocabulary.

What 動うごかす moves is surprisingly broad. Furniture, a car, your body — sure. But also hearts: 人ひとの心こころを動うごかす (to move someone's heart). And entire organizations: 会社かいしゃを動うごかす (to run a company — literally, to make it move). The word doesn't distinguish between physical and metaphorical motion. If you're the force making something change position or state, it's 動うごかす.

Particles: 動うごかす takes を — the thing you move is your direct object. 机つくえを動うごかす (move the desk).

Potential が動うごかせる: が marks what can be moved. 一いち人ひとでこの岩が動うごかせる？ (can you move this rock alone?).

Passive に動うごかされる: に marks who moved it. 彼かれのスピーチに動うごかされた (I was moved by his speech).

Causative に…を動うごかさせる: に marks the person, を marks the thing. 部へ下したに荷物にもつを動うごかさせる (make the subordinate move the luggage).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 動く (うごく)
Dictionaryうごかすを
Negativeうごかさないを
Politeうごかしますを
Polite negうごかしませんを
Pastうごかしたを
Polite pastうごかしましたを
て-formうごかしてを
Volitionalうごかそうを
Polite volうごかしましょうを
Passiveうごかされるに
Causativeうごかさせるに／を
Caus-passうごかさせられるに
Potentialうごかせるが
Imperativeうごかせを
Imp なさいうごかしなさいを
Cond ～ばうごかせばを
Cond ～たらうごかしたらを

meaning: to break (down), be broken, fall apart
lesson:
The kanji 壊かい has 土つち (earth) on the left and a complex right side that originally depicted a garment being torn apart. Earth crumbling, fabric tearing — destruction from within. That's exactly what 壊こわれる describes: something breaks, falls apart, ceases to function — and nobody is named as the cause. The machine broke. The relationship fell apart. The plan collapsed. The thing just... stopped working.

Pair: 壊こわれる is intransitive — things break on their own. 壊こわす (こわす) is transitive — you break them. パソコンが壊こわれた (the computer broke) vs パソコンを壊こわした (I broke the computer). Japanese speakers instinctively reach for the intransitive version when they don't want to assign blame — which is most of the time. Saying 壊こわした puts someone at fault. Saying 壊こわれた lets the object take responsibility for its own demise.

壊こわれる goes beyond physical objects. 体からだが壊こわれる (one's health breaks down). 約束やくそくが壊こわれる is possible but rare — for promises, 破れる is more natural. For electronics and appliances, 壊こわれる is the default word. Your washing machine, your phone, your car — when they stop working, they 壊こわれた.

Particles: 壊こわれる takes が — the broken thing is the subject, not an object. 時とき計が壊こわれた (the watch broke).

Causative を／に壊こわれさせる: making or letting something break. を for coercive — 機械きかいを壊こわれさせた (caused the machine to break). に leans permissive.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 壊す (こわす)
Dictionaryこわれるが
Negativeこわれないが
Politeこわれますが
Polite negこわれませんが
Pastこわれたが
Polite pastこわれましたが
て-formこわれてが
Volitionalこわれようが
Polite volこわれましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばこわれればが
Cond ～たらこわれたらが

復ふく (return, repeat) + 習しゅう (learn, practice) = going back over what you've learned. The word assumes you've already studied the material once — this is the return trip. Its natural opposite is 予習よしゅう (よしゅう, preparation/preview), where 予よ means "before" — studying material before it's taught. In Japanese school culture, the pair is drilled into students early: 予習よしゅうと復習ふくしゅうをしなさい (do your prep work and your review).

As a する-noun, it pairs directly with する: 復習ふくしゅうする (to review). You'll also see 復習ふくしゅうをする with the を, which is equally natural. The thing you're reviewing takes を: 漢かん字じを復習ふくしゅうする.

If you're using Anki alongside this app, 復習ふくしゅう is literally what you're doing every time you open it. The word is your daily practice, named.

The kanji 眉まゆ is a pictograph — one of the few body-part kanji where you can still see the original drawing. The top portion represents the eyebrow hairs arching over the 目め (eye) at the bottom. It's a face in miniature: brow over eye.

まゆ shows up in some vivid expressions. 眉まゆをひそめる (to furrow one's brows — expressing displeasure or doubt). 眉まゆ唾 (まゆつば, literally "eyebrow spit" — something dubious, fishy). That last one comes from an old belief that wetting your eyebrows with spit would protect you from being deceived by foxes. The word survived long after the superstition died.

客きゃく covers three English words that feel quite different — customer, guest, visitor — and this tells you something important about how Japanese thinks about the relationship. Whether you're buying something at a store, visiting someone's home, or attending an event, you're the 客きゃく: the person who has come from outside and is being received. The common thread isn't commerce or hospitality specifically — it's the host-guest dynamic.

The polite prefix お transforms it: お客きゃくさん (おきゃくさん) is how you'd actually refer to a customer or guest in most situations. Using bare 客きゃく sounds blunt — fine in compounds like 客きゃく室 (きゃくしつ, guest room) or 乗客じょうきゃく (じょうきゃく, passenger), but when talking about actual people, the お and さん are practically mandatory.

The kanji itself: 宀うかんむり (roof) over 各 (each, every). Someone who comes under your roof — a person arriving from elsewhere. The "each" component hints at the individual nature of the visit: each guest is a separate event of receiving.

Three syllables for a desk: tsu-ku-e. The kanji 机つくえ is 木き (tree/wood) with 几き (a small table) — wood shaped into a flat surface you work on. 几き by itself is an ancient character showing the side profile of a low table with two legs. Stack wood on that concept and you get 机つくえ.

机 vs テーブル: Both are flat surfaces, but they live in different rooms. 机つくえ is where you study or work — it implies sitting down and doing something productive. A student's desk, an office desk. テーブル is where you eat, set things down, or gather around. Dining table, coffee table. If someone says 机つくえに向かう (to face one's desk), they're about to work. Nobody says that about a テーブル.

The Japanese bath is not a shower. 風呂ふろ means the tub itself, the act of soaking in it, and the entire bathing ritual — washing outside the tub first, then lowering yourself into hot water that the whole family will share. The tub isn't for getting clean (that happens at the washing area). The tub is for soaking. When someone says お風呂ふろに入はいる, they mean the full experience.

The kanji are curious: 風かぜ (wind) and 呂 (spine, backbone — but here used phonetically). The word's origins are debated. One theory traces it to the steam rooms of medieval Japan, where hot wind (風かぜ) was the medium. Another connects it to a specific type of steam bath structure. Either way, the kanji are ateji — chosen for sound, not meaning.

お風呂ふろ with the polite お is standard in daily conversation. Bare 風呂ふろ sounds slightly rough. The compound 風呂場ふろば (ふろば) means the bathroom itself — the room where the tub lives.

One syllable, one kanji, one specific meaning: 湯ゆ is hot water. Not warm water, not water in general — hot water. The kanji has 氵さんずい (water radical) on the left and 昜 (rising sun/heat) on the right. Water plus heat. Elegant.

This distinction matters in Japanese more than it does in English. 水みず (みず) is cold or room-temperature water. 湯ゆ is water that's been heated. They're not the same substance in the Japanese mind. お湯ゆを沸わかす (boil water — literally "heat the hot water," which sounds redundant in English but isn't in Japanese: you're bringing 湯 into existence).

湯ゆ connects to the entire culture of bathing and hot springs. 銭湯せんとう (せんとう, public bath — "coin hot-water"). 温泉おんせん uses different kanji but the concept is the same world. When you see 湯ゆ in a place name — and you will, all over Japan — it marks a spot with hot springs.

ぬるい is the temperature nobody wants. Not hot, not cold — disappointingly in between. Your bath has gone ぬるい. Your coffee is ぬるい. In a culture that takes both hot baths and cold beer seriously, lukewarm is a failure state.

The word has a figurative edge too. ぬるい考かんがえ means soft, weak, half-hearted thinking — lacking the intensity the situation demands. A coach might call a team's effort ぬるい. It carries genuine contempt: you're neither one thing nor the other, and that's worse than being wrong.

Sound: Say nu-ru-i slowly. The ぬ has a heavy, flat quality — there's no sharpness to it, no energy. The word sounds like what it describes.

Dictionaryぬるい
Negativeぬるくない
Pastぬるかった
Past negぬるくなかった
Adverbぬるく
て-formぬるくて

A clean compound: 風邪かぜ (かぜ, a cold) + 薬ぐすり (くすり, medicine) = cold medicine. Notice the く voices to ぐ at the join — this is rendaku, the voicing phenomenon that happens when two words fuse into a compound. くすり becomes ぐすり. You'll see rendaku everywhere once you start looking: 花はな火 (はなび, not はなひ), 青あお空そら (あおぞら, not あおそら).

In Japan, 風邪薬かぜぐすり is available at any pharmacy (薬局やっきょく, やっきょく) without a prescription. Brands like Pabron and Lulu are household names. Telling someone 風邪かぜ薬を飲のんだ？ (did you take cold medicine?) is a standard piece of Japanese concern-showing.

靴くつ (くつ, shoes) + 下した (した, under/below) = the thing under your shoes. Socks are shoe-unders. Japanese names for clothing are often this literal: 靴下くつした tells you exactly where the garment goes.

The word matters practically because of the shoe-removal culture. In homes, schools, temples, many restaurants, and some workplaces, you take your shoes off at the entrance. Your 靴下くつした are suddenly on display. Showing up with holes in them is a minor social embarrassment that Japanese people are quietly vigilant about. The expression 靴下したくつしたに穴が開あいている (there's a hole in my socks) carries a small note of dread.

One of the oldest European loanwords in Japanese. Portuguese traders brought tobacco to Japan in the 1500s, and the Portuguese word tabaco came with it. It arrived centuries before Japan started using katakana for foreign words, so it stuck as hiragana — or sometimes the kanji 煙草たばこ (literally "smoke grass"), though that's becoming old-fashioned.

In daily use, たばこ almost always means a cigarette rather than tobacco in general. たばこを吸すう (to smoke a cigarette — literally "inhale tobacco"). The counter-phrase: たばこをやめる (to quit smoking). Japan's relationship with smoking has shifted dramatically — most public spaces are now non-smoking, and designated smoking areas (喫煙所しょ, きつえんじょ) are increasingly small and isolated. But the word itself remains common.

Straight English: "ice coffee" rendered in katakana. Japan didn't just borrow the word — it arguably invented the concept. Iced coffee became widespread in Japanese kissaten (coffee houses) in the 1920s and 30s, decades before it caught on in the West. When you order アイスコーヒー in Japan, you typically get black iced coffee with a small pitcher of simple syrup and cream on the side.

In casual speech, this gets shortened to アイコ — a common Japanese abbreviation pattern where long katakana words get clipped to their first two mora. パソコン from パーソナルコンピューター, エアコン from エアコンディショナー. Same instinct.

Another Portuguese legacy, probably from tempero (seasoning) or temporas (the Catholic Ember Days, when meat was forbidden and fried fish was eaten instead). Portuguese missionaries in 16th-century Nagasaki brought their frying technique; the Japanese perfected it into something unrecognizable from its origins. Modern tempura — a whisper-light batter, barely there, shattering on contact — is entirely Japanese in execution.

The writing is interesting: 天てん (heaven) + ぷら in hiragana. Sometimes you'll see the full kanji 天麩羅てんぷら, but that's ateji — kanji forced onto a foreign word for their sounds, not their meanings. Most people just write 天ぷらてんぷら or てんぷら.

Know that 天ぷらてんぷら in a Japanese context doesn't mean "fried stuff" generically. It specifically means items dipped in a light egg-and-flour batter and deep-fried. Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet) and karaage (fried chicken) are not tempura — different technique, different word.

The kanji 肉にく is a pictograph of a piece of meat — the inner strokes represent the grain or sinew of flesh. It doubled as the source of the 月にくづき-shaped radical you see in dozens of body-part kanji: 腕 (arm), 胸むね (chest), 腰 (waist), 肩かた (shoulder). That radical isn't actually "moon" in those characters — it's a simplified 肉にく. Whenever you see 月にくづき on the left side of a kanji related to the body, think meat, not moon.

On its own, 肉にく usually means meat for eating. 肉にくを焼く (grill meat). When someone in Japan says just 肉にく without specifying the animal, they often default to either beef or pork depending on the region — beef in Kansai (Osaka area), pork in Kanto (Tokyo area). Regional identity runs deep, even in meat.

The kanji say 昨夜さくや — "previous night" — but the reading is ゆうべ, which comes from 夕ゆうべ (evening). This is one of those cases where kanji and reading come from completely different words that got married. The Chinese-derived reading さくや also exists and sounds more formal or literary. In conversation, ゆうべ is the natural choice.

ゆうべ covers roughly from dusk through the night. It's flexible: ゆうべ何なに食たべた？ could mean dinner time or late at night, depending on context. Compare with 昨日にちきのうの夜よる (きのうのよる), which means the same thing but sounds slightly more specific — you're pointing at the nighttime portion of yesterday rather than just saying "last night" as a unit.

meaning: to be in fashion, be popular
lesson:
The kanji 流行りゅうこう (りゅうこう) means "trend" or "fashion" as a noun. But read the same kanji as a verb — はやる — and it means something is currently in style, spreading, catching on. Diseases also はやる: インフルエンザが流行はやっている (the flu is going around). Fashion and epidemics use the same verb because both spread through populations. The kanji literally mean "flowing movement" — things moving through a crowd.

Pay attention to the tense. 流りゅう行いっている (ongoing) is far more common than the dictionary form, because you're usually describing a current state: something is popular right now. 今いま、この歌が流行いっている (this song is popular right now).

Particles: 流行はやる takes が — the popular thing is the subject. 新あたらしいゲームが流行いっている (a new game is trending).

Causative を／に流りゅう行いらせる: making something popular. を marks the thing you spread. 新あたらしいダンスを流行いらせた (made a new dance popular).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryはやるが
Negativeはやらないが
Politeはやりますが
Polite negはやりませんが
Pastはやったが
Polite pastはやりましたが
て-formはやってが
Volitionalはやろうが
Polite volはやりましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばはやればが
Cond ～たらはやったらが

This is a compound verb: 連つれる (つれる, to lead/take along) in its て-form + くる (to come). The て+くる pattern means doing something toward the speaker — bringing rather than taking. Its mirror is 連つれていく (to take someone away from here).

People only. 連つれてくる is for bringing living things — people, pets, occasionally livestock. For objects, you use 持もってくる (もってくる) instead. 友達ともだちを連れてきた (I brought a friend). 犬いぬを連れてきた (I brought the dog). But: 本ほんを持もってきた (I brought a book) — not 連れて.

The conjugation follows くる's irregular pattern. The 連れて portion doesn't change — all the inflection happens on くる: 連つれてくる → 連れんれてきた → 連れんれてこない → 連れんれてきます.

Particles: を marks the person being brought. 彼女おんなかのじょを連れてくる (bring her along).

Passive に連れんれてこられる: に marks the bringer. 親に連れんれてこられた (I was brought by my parents).

Causative に…を連れんれてこさせる: making someone bring a person. 部へ下したに客きゃくを連れてこさせた (had my subordinate bring the client).

Irregular (くる)他動詞
Dictionaryつれてくるを
Negativeつれてこないを
Politeつれてきますを
Polite negつれてきませんを
Pastつれてきたを
Polite pastつれてきましたを
て-formつれてきてを
Volitionalつれてこようを
Polite volつれてきましょうを
Passiveつれてこられるに
Causativeつれてこさせるに／を
Caus-passつれてこさせられるに
Potentialつれてこられるを
Imperativeつれてこいを
Imp なさいつれてきなさいを
Cond ～ばつれてくればを
Cond ～たらつれてきたらを

You already know 方かた read as ほう (direction, way). Read as かた, it's a polite word for "person" — the upgrade from 人ひと (ひと) when you need to be respectful. あの方かた (that person — polite) vs あの人ひと (that person — neutral). In customer service, announcements, and formal speech, 方かた replaces 人ひと almost everywhere.

お客きゃく様の中なかに、お医者様はいらっしゃいますか？ — that kind of formal announcement would never say 人ひと. But in slightly less formal contexts: あの方かたはどなたですか (who is that person?). The choice between 人ひと and 方かた is one of the first register decisions a learner has to make, and getting it right signals awareness of social distance.

The kanji 零れい has 雨あめ (rain) on top and 令れい (command, order) on the bottom. The original Chinese meaning was "a fine drizzle" — tiny droplets falling, almost nothing. From there it drifted to mean "fragmentary, falling to pieces," and eventually "nothing at all." Zero as the logical endpoint of diminishing.

In practice, you'll hear ゼロ (from English) at least as often as れい. The split: れい is used in formal contexts — phone numbers read officially, temperatures, mathematical notation. ゼロ is casual and emphatic — 可能かのう性はゼロ (the probability is zero). Phone numbers in daily conversation often use ゼロ too. Both are correct; register determines the choice.

雲くも is 雨あめ (rain) over 云うん (an ancient character meaning "to say" — used here for its swirling, rising shape). Rain hasn't fallen yet — it's still gathered above, curling upward. A cloud is pre-rain.

Watch out: くも is also the word for spider (蜘蛛). Same sound, wildly different kanji, totally unrelated words. Context prevents confusion — 空そらにくもが出でてきた is obviously clouds; 部へ屋やにくもがいた is obviously a spider. But it's the kind of homophone that might trip you up in listening practice.

The compound 雲くも海うみ (うんかい, sea of clouds) describes the view from a mountain when clouds fill the valleys below like an ocean. It's one of those Japanese words that compresses a whole visual experience into two syllables.

The kanji 空そら sits at a remarkable intersection. Read そら, it's the sky — the visible dome overhead. Read から, it means empty. Read くう in compounds, it means air, void, or sky. Three readings, three meanings, all circling the same idea: the vast open nothing above us.

The Buddhist concept of 空そら (くう, emptiness/void) is philosophically foundational — the idea that all things lack inherent existence. But for vocabulary purposes, the reading you need now is そら: the sky you look up at. 空そらが青あおい (the sky is blue). 空そらを見み上うえげる (to look up at the sky).

The connection between sky and emptiness is intuitive: look up and what's there? Nothing — and everything. The kanji captures both.

人ひと (people) + 気き (spirit, energy, feeling) = the energy of people, the crowd's enthusiasm — popularity. When something has 人気にんき, people are drawn to it. The word works as a noun that modifies with の or がある: 人気にんきのある店みせ or 人気にんきがある店みせ (a popular restaurant).

人気にんきがある vs 人気にんきがない is a simple binary that comes up constantly — popular or not. このバンドは若わか者に人気にんきがある (this band is popular with young people). Notice the に marking the group that finds it popular.

Reading trap: 人気にんき read as ひとけ means something completely different: signs of human presence. 人気にんきのない道みち (ひとけのないみち — a deserted road, a road with no sign of people). Same kanji, opposite feeling. Context makes it clear.

You've seen お兄あにさん (おにいさん) as the standard way to refer to someone else's older brother. Drop the お and you get 兄あにさん — still polite, but now it's your own brother, used when talking to him directly or referring to him in a context that doesn't need maximum formality.

The family-term system in Japanese has this layered structure: 兄あに (あに) is the humblest form — you'd use it to describe your own brother to outsiders. 兄あにさん (にいさん) is what you might call him at home. お兄あにさん (おにいさん) is the full polite version — used for other people's brothers, or as an address term for any young man you don't know well.

In anime and manga, you'll hear にいさん or the more intimate にいちゃん constantly. Real families vary — some use お兄あにちゃん, some use first names, some use 兄あにさん. The word tells you about the speaker's relationship and register.

The sister counterpart to 兄あにさん. Same structure: 姉あね (あね, humble) → 姉あねさん (ねえさん, familiar-polite) → お姉あねさん (おねえさん, full polite).

ねえさん doubles as a casual address term for any youngish woman in certain contexts — calling a waitress in a traditional izakaya, addressing a slightly older woman you know casually. It's warm but can feel old-fashioned. The variant ねえちゃん is rougher and more familiar — a little brother calling his sister, or a man addressing a young woman informally (which can sound rude depending on context).

The kanji 姉あね is 女おんな (woman) + 市し (market — used for sound here). Not much visual story to it, but the word itself is deeply embedded in Japanese family vocabulary.

平へい (peace, flat) + 成せい (become, achieve) = "achieving peace." This was the era name (年とし号, ねんごう) for Emperor Akihito's reign, from January 8, 1989 to April 30, 2019. Japan uses both the Western calendar and its own era system simultaneously, and official documents, government forms, and many daily contexts still use era years: 平成31年 = 2019.

The current era is 令和わ (れいわ, since May 2019). Before 平成 was 昭和わ (しょうわ, 1926-1989). Japanese people often identify generations by era — 平へい成せい生うまれ (born in Heisei) carries cultural connotations the way "millennial" does in English. To convert: Heisei year + 1988 = Western year. So 平成10年 = 1998.

The kanji 毎まい is underappreciated. Look at it: it's 母はは (mother) inside a repeating frame. The original meaning was "each time, every instance" — the idea of a mother who produces offspring again and again, each one a repetition. From that came the modern prefix meaning "every": 毎まい日にち (every day), 毎まい週しゅう (every week), 毎まい年とし (every year), and here, 毎まい月つき.

Two readings exist: まいつき (hybrid — Chinese まい + Japanese つき) and まいげつ (fully Chinese-derived). まいつき is more common in speech. The word floats in sentences without a particle: 毎まい月つき家いえ賃を払はらう (I pay rent every month). 毎まい月つきの給料 (monthly salary) uses の to modify a noun.

The 毎まい pattern is one of those rare corners of Japanese that's completely regular and predictable. Enjoy it while it lasts.

The tricky part isn't the meaning — it's the reading. 半はん日にち can be はんにち or はんじつ. The first is standard in conversation. The second appears in formal or written contexts. What you will not hear is はんひ — the 日にち takes its on-reading here, not its kun-reading.

半はん日にち in practice means "a significant chunk of a day" rather than a precise 12 hours. 半はん日にちかかった (it took half a day) almost always carries a note of frustration — that was too long. 半はん日にちコース (half-day course) and 半はん日にち休やすみ (half-day off) are real categories in Japanese scheduling, usually meaning either morning-only or afternoon-only. The kanji 半はん itself is a visual: a vertical line cutting something in two. Half.

Here's where the reading matters more than usual. 半はん月つき read as はんつき = half a month, about two weeks. 半はん月つき read as はんげつ = a half moon, the crescent shape in the sky. Same kanji, completely different images. The time-word uses the native Japanese つき; the astronomical term uses the Chinese-derived げつ. If you get the reading wrong, you're talking about the sky when you meant the calendar.

半はん月つき前まえに引っ越した (I moved two weeks ago). あと半はん月つきで卒業そつぎょうだ (graduation is in half a month). In daily life, 半はん月つき sits in a useful gap — it's what you say when 二に週しゅう間ま (two weeks) feels too precise and しばらく (a while) feels too vague.
